title: Data Sources and Cleaning
text:
To understand the 2010s music landscape, we utilized the MusicOSet dataset, which provides an open and enhanced collection of musical elements suitable for data mining. Our analysis relied on three primary files:

hits_dataset.csv: Spotify audio features for 11,959 songs that appeared on the Billboard Hot 100.
nonhits_dataset.csv: Spotify audio features for 899,068 songs by “hit artists” (artists that had at least one charting song) that did not chart.
artists.csv: Metadata for 11,518 artists, including their associated genres.

title: Data Sources and Cleaning
text:
To understand the 2010s music landscape, we utilized the MusicOSet dataset, which provides an open and enhanced collection of musical elements suitable for data mining. Our analysis relied on three primary files:

hits_dataset.csv: Spotify audio features for 11,959 songs that appeared on the Billboard Hot 100.
nonhits_dataset.csv: Spotify audio features for 899,068 songs by “hit artists” (artists that had at least one charting song) that did not chart.
artists.csv: Metadata for 11,518 artists, including their associated genres.

title: Data Sources and Cleaning
text:
The Cleaning Process

1. Broadening the Genre Categories
In the artists dataset, the main_genre entries were too specific/niche for effective usage. We used keyword searches to group these into broader categories such as ‘Pop’, ‘Hip Hop/Rap’, ‘Rock/Metal’, and ‘Electronic/Dance’, among others. Any genres with fewer than 100 associated artists after the combined dataset was made were moved into an ‘Other’ category to maintain statistical significance.


2. Standardizing the 2010s Scope
We applied several filters to our hits and nonhits datasets:

Timeframe: We only kept tracks with a release date between 2010 and 2019, since this is the scope of interest. Since the provided release_date was based on when the song was uploaded to Spotify, artists whose older music (typically made before the streaming era) was added to Spotify during this period were also removed.
Consistency: We dropped songs by artists who did not have at least one hit during this specific decade (to create two comparable datasets where popularity of the arist during this period is less of a factor).
Outlier/Missing Artist Removal: We identified and removed certain “artists” who uploaded extremely long mixes of multiple songs (like workout mixes), which skewed our duration_ms column. We also removed artists that were not found in the artists dataset, and thus wouldn’t have been able to be assigned to a genre.



3. Balancing the Dataset
The raw data was heavily skewed toward non-hits (nearly 900,000 non-hits vs. 12,000 hits). To create a fair classification model (one that wouldn’t succeed by guessing “not a hit” everytime), we:

Took a random sample of non-hits equal to the number of hits.
This resulted in a final combined_music_data set of 7,392 songs (3,696 hits and 3,696 non-hits).



4. Normalization
To ensure our model wasn’t influenced by the different scale for certain measurements, we normalized multiple key variables—loudness, tempo, and duration_ms—onto a scale of 0 to 1.

title: Data Sources and Cleaning
text:
Final Dataset Structure and Codebook
After cleaning, our dataset consisted of 7,392 observations across 16 variables. Below is the summary of our primary explanatory variables after normalization, as well as what they mean and our interpretation of their summary statistics. Unless stated otherwise, 1 indicates a high level of the variable and 0 indicates a low level.











Variable
Description
Mean
Median
Std. Dev.
Interpretation




duration_ms
Track length, standardized (0 to 1).
0.047
0.045
0.019
Mean and median are nearly identical, showing a highly symmetrical, consistent distribution of track lengths.


acousticness
Confidence measure of whether a track is acoustic.
0.384
0.251
0.362
The median is significantly lower than the mean, indicating a right-skewed distribution dominated by non-acoustic tracks.


danceability
Suitability for dancing based on tempo, rhythm, and beat.
0.573
0.585
0.156
High central tendency (~0.58) suggests the dataset consistently favors rhythmically stable, danceable tracks.


energy
Measure of intensity and activity.
0.546
0.561
0.254
Tracks generally lean toward higher intensity, with a balanced distribution around the 0.56 mark.


instrumentalness
Likelihood that a track contains no vocals.
0.185
0.000
0.336
A median of 0 confirms that the vast majority of tracks are vocal-heavy, with high SD driven by a few outliers.


liveness
Presence of an audience in the recording.
0.217
0.126
0.208
Low median relative to mean suggests a few live tracks pull the average up, but studio recordings are the standard.


loudness
Overall loudness in decibels (dB), standardized (0 to 1).
0.822
0.845
0.087
Very high median and low SD indicate tracks are extremely and consistently compressed at high volumes.


speechiness
Presence of spoken words in a track.
0.089
0.044
0.128
Low median confirms the samples are almost exclusively melodic music rather than spoken-word content.


valence
Musical positiveness.
0.471
0.449
0.244
Both measures sit near the center, reflecting a diverse emotional range.


tempo
Speed of the track (BPM), standardized (0 to 1).
0.489
0.486
0.120
Values are tightly clustered around the center, indicating a lack of extreme outliers in speed.


explicit
Binary indicator for explicit lyrical content.
0.117
0.000
0.321
A mean of ~12% indicates that while present, explicit tracks are the minority in this dataset.



Not Included:

key: estimated overall key of the track, maps pitches to integers using standard Pitch Class notation (-1 = no key detected, 0 = key of C, …, 11 = key of B).
mode: indicator of what kind of scale was used (1 = major scale, 0 = minor scale)
genre: genre that the main artist of a track is associated with
hit: boolean indicating if a song reached the Billboard Hot 100

title: Introduction
text:
In the music industry, there is a long-standing debate: is a song’s success driven by its quantifiable musical characteristics, or is it a product of “unmeasurables” like marketing, catchiness, pure luck, and timing, among others?
Our project seeks to address this by analyzing a comprehensive sample of hit and non-hit songs released between 2010 and 2019. We define a “hit” specifically as any song that made an appearance on the Billboard Hot 100 at some point after its release.


We chose to focus on the 2010s for several reasons:

Formative Years: This decade represents the childhood and teenage years for many in our generation, meaning the music resonates with many of us.
Lasting Impact: The music from this era was exceptionally high-quality and “timeless”, with many tracks still charting or receiving many streams today.
Shifting Trends: The criteria for what makes a song “successful” appeared to undergo significant shifts during this decade compared to those before.




The primary objective of this study is to predict the charting success of 2010s songs based on their audio characteristics. We aimed to build a classification model capable of differentiating hit songs from non-hit songs with at least 50% accuracy (better than just random guessing!).



Going into our modeling phase, we developed two sets of expectations regarding Spotify’s audio features:

Predictors of Success: We hypothesized that higher danceability, valence (musical positiveness), and loudness would significantly increase a song’s likelihood of becoming a hit. We also expected the genres pop and hip hop/rap to be the driving forces of success since these genres seemed to really take over the mainstream during this period. Conversely, we expected lower duration, acousticness, speechiness, and instrumentalness to be associated with chart success, as well as genres like rock, folk, and classical.
Neutral Elements: We predicted that features like key, tempo, mode, and whether a song is explicit would have little to no measurable effect on a song’s probability of charting.


Next, we will dive into the Data and Cleaning process to show how we narrowed down nearly a million observations into a clean, balanced dataset for our analysis and modeling.

title: Introduction
text:
In the music industry, there is a long-standing debate: is a song’s success driven by its quantifiable musical characteristics, or is it a product of “unmeasurables” like marketing, catchiness, pure luck, and timing, among others?
Our project seeks to address this by analyzing a comprehensive sample of hit and non-hit songs released between 2010 and 2019. We define a “hit” specifically as any song that made an appearance on the Billboard Hot 100 at some point after its release.


We chose to focus on the 2010s for several reasons:

Formative Years: This decade represents the childhood and teenage years for many in our generation, meaning the music resonates with many of us.
Lasting Impact: The music from this era was exceptionally high-quality and “timeless”, with many tracks still charting or receiving many streams today.
Shifting Trends: The criteria for what makes a song “successful” appeared to undergo significant shifts during this decade compared to those before.




The primary objective of this study is to predict the charting success of 2010s songs based on their audio characteristics. We aimed to build a classification model capable of differentiating hit songs from non-hit songs with at least 50% accuracy (better than just random guessing!).



Going into our modeling phase, we developed two sets of expectations regarding Spotify’s audio features:

Predictors of Success: We hypothesized that higher danceability, valence (musical positiveness), and loudness would significantly increase a song’s likelihood of becoming a hit. We also expected the genres pop and hip hop/rap to be the driving forces of success since these genres seemed to really take over the mainstream during this period. Conversely, we expected lower duration, acousticness, speechiness, and instrumentalness to be associated with chart success, as well as genres like rock, folk, and classical.
Neutral Elements: We predicted that features like key, tempo, mode, and whether a song is explicit would have little to no measurable effect on a song’s probability of charting.


Next, we will dive into the Data and Cleaning process to show how we narrowed down nearly a million observations into a clean, balanced dataset for our analysis and modeling.

title: Modeling Results
text:
Our primary goal was to differentiate between hit and non-hit songs with better than 50% accuracy. Both our LASSO and Ridge models successfully exceeded this threshold, achieving approximately 69% accuracy on the test data when genre was included as a predictor.
When running our models, we had originally set a constant seed number when choosing the subset of the nonhits dataset to use. The exploratory data analysis results thus far reflect those resulting from our seed seelction of 67. However, we wanted to know how the model would perform when a different subset was chosen. For this reason, the figures below reflect the results from an average of five randomly-chosen seed numbers.


Whether we used the L1 (LASSO) or L2 (Ridge) penalty, the performance was fairly consistent:










Performance Metric
LASSO (w/ genre)
Ridge (w/ genre)
LASSO (w/o genre)
Ridge (w/o genre)




Accuracy
69.23%
69.42%
66.49%
66.42%


F1 Score
0.72
0.73
0.71
0.70


ROC-AUC
0.75
0.75
0.73
0.73



The models identified several audio features that strongly influenced the likelihood of a song becoming a hit. The table below demonstrates the top 10 most influential predictors when genre was included across the two models:





Feature

It is evident that genre is the main factor driving our predictions. For this reason, we might expect model accuracy to decrease significantly when genre is not included. Interestingly, removing “Genre” as a predictor only decreased the accuracy by around 3%, suggesting that the raw audio characteristics themselves carry significant weight regardless of how the song is categorized. The table below demonstrates the top 7 most influential predictors when genre was not included across the two models (the other predictors had very small coefficients or sent to 0):






Feature
LASSO (average)





instrumentalness

loudness

speechiness

Model

66.42%
0.70
0.73






While marketing and luck undoubtedly play a role, our models suggest that approximately 69% of a song’s chart success can be explained by track characteristics. Key takeaways from our LASSO and Ridge analysis include:

Even when we removed the Genre tags, the models only lost ~3% accuracy. This indicates that the characteristics of the audio help us to predict song success without genre labels.
Instrumentalness was one of the most powerful negative predictors across the genre-blind models. In the 2010s, songs that reached mainstream success were often characterized as “vocal-heavy” and lacking significant instrumental presence.
The positive coefficients for Loudness and Explicit (True) suggest that the 2010s favored more aggressive and louder tracks. Conversely, the negative coefficients for Liveness and Speechiness highlight that the hit songs in the 2010s were typically not live recordings or spoken word tracks.

While our model isn’t perfect, a 69% success rate proves that there is indeed a “tangible formula” to 2010s hits that goes beyond simple marketing luck.

title: Discussion
text:
Our analysis successfully demonstrated that 2010s chart success is not purely random. By consistently achieving a 69% accuracy rate, we proved that a significant portion of what makes a “hit” is rooted in the characteristics of the audio—most notably the genre, danceability, loudness, and instrumentalness.
However, the remaining 31% of “unexplained” success indicates unmeasurables still play a significant role: marketing, social media virality, and good timing, among others.

title: Discussion
text:
Our analysis successfully demonstrated that 2010s chart success is not purely random. By consistently achieving a 69% accuracy rate, we proved that a significant portion of what makes a “hit” is rooted in the characteristics of the audio—most notably the genre, danceability, loudness, and instrumentalness.
However, the remaining 31% of “unexplained” success indicates unmeasurables still play a significant role: marketing, social media virality, and good timing, among others.

title: Discussion
text:
Limitations of the Study
While our models were robust, we identified several factors that likely hindered our results:

The “Remaster” Bias: Our dataset included some remastered versions of older songs released between 2010 and 2019. These tracks often follow the musical “formula” of a different decade, which may have confused the model’s understanding of the 2010s-specific trends.
Incorrect Labeling: During our exploration of the data we noted that hit songs uploaded by other people were labeled as hits even if the artist that uploaded the song was not the original artist. This includes DJ mixes, compilations, and “slowed and reverb” mixes. This poses an issue because the reuploader’s discography was included in our datasets even though they did not have a hit themselves. It was difficult to detect these inconsistencies and although we tried to omit as many of those as possible, it was likely that some remained in the final data set.
Streaming Metadata: Some songs had incorrect release dates due to when they were uploaded to streaming platforms, rather than when they actually debuted on the charts.
Data Imbalance by Genre: While we grouped genres to maintain significance, certain niche genres had significantly fewer observations, potentially biasing the estimates for those specific categories. Furthermore, some of the genres had a smaller group of artists that resided in them, making the genres more of a variable representing that collection of artists rather than the genre itself.

title: Discussion
text:
Future Work
To build upon these findings, “The Danish Club” suggests several areas for further investigation:

Collaboration Effects: Do collaborations significantly boost a song’s probability of success regardless of its audio features?
Artist Popularity: Controlling for an artist’s popularity at the time the song was released could greatly enhance our predictions.
Modern Testing: We would like to test this 2010s-trained model on songs from the 2020s to see if the “formula” has fundamentally shifted in the new era, although the data sources stopped collecting data in late 2019.
Deeper Genre Analysis: Expanding the dataset to include more balanced samples of niche genres to see if the predictors of success change (e.g., is “loudness” as important in Country as it is in EDM?).



Final Takeaway
While we may not have discovered a perfect formula for success, we have successfully identified key factors that likely influence song popularity in the 2010s. Genre was the dominant force, with Soundtrack/Theater, Country, and Soul/R&B/Funk indicating that songs were more likely to be hits, whereas Comedy/Entertainment pointed towards a nonhit. When genre was ommitted, we got a clearer view of how the audio characteristics played a role in the song’s success, while only losing about 3% in accuracy. Instrumental, live, and spoken word tracks strongly indicated less success and loud, explicit, and happier songs indicated more success.

title: Exploratory Data Analysis
text:
To understand the characteristics of hit versus non-hit songs in our dataset, we conducted a comprehensive exploratory data analysis (EDA) of the audio features from Spotify. This section reveals important patterns in our data and helps validate our initial hypotheses about what makes a song successful.


We began by examining whether certain genres were more likely to produce chart-topping hits. The results, shown below, demonstrate clear variation in success rates across genres for :



genre
n
n_hits
hit_rate




Soundtrack/Theater
914
84
9.19


Country
7923
584
7.37


Soul/R&B/Funk
1812
109
6.02


Hip Hop/Rap
11471
610
5.32


Pop
25216
1305
5.18


Electronic/Dance
2833
104
3.67


Rock/Metal
9900
344
3.47


Indie
4716
104
2.21


Other
6548
143
2.18


Easy Listening
18189
281
1.54


Comedy/Entertainment
4548
16
0.35


Children’s Music
6795
12
0.18



Soundtrack/Theater, Country, and Soul/R&B/Funk songs had the highest hit rates, ranging from ~6-9%. In contrast, Children’s Music and Comedy/Entertainment had the lowest rates, below 0.5% each. This suggests that certain genres have intrinsically different likelihood of achieving mainstream success. Interestingly, Pop and Hip Hop/Rap—our expectations of the most dominant genres during this period—had a moderate hit rate of ~5% each, possibly indicating an oversaturation in the market.



Next, we investigated how key audio characteristics differed between hits and non-hits over the decade. We hypothesized that higher energy, danceability, and valence would be associated with hits, while lower acousticness would favor commercial success.


The chart below shows the average energy levels for hits and non-hits across the 2010-2019 period:





Key observation: Hit songs consistently maintained higher energy levels than non-hits throughout the decade (ranging from 0.60–0.72 for hits vs. 0.43–0.61 for non-hits). The gap between hits and non-hits was most pronounced in the early 2010s and gradually narrowed toward 2019, suggesting that energy became a less distinctive marker of success over time. This preliminary finding aligns well with our first hypothesis that energy is a positive predictor of chart success.



Valence—Spotify’s measure of musical positiveness or cheerfulness—also showed interesting trends:





Hit songs consistently scored higher on valence, though the difference was more subtle than with energy. Hit songs averaged around 0.48–0.65 in valence, while non-hits averaged 0.40–0.52. Notably, both hit and non-hit songs showed a sharp increase in valence in 2019, suggesting a broader industry shift toward more upbeat music during that year. This also supports our hypothesis that positive, cheerful music performs better commercially.



Danceability, perhaps the most intuitive predictor of radio-friendly hits, showed strong separation between the two groups:





Hit songs were notably more danceable, with averages ranging from 0.59–0.67, compared to non-hits at 0.52–0.61. Interestingly, danceability showed more year-to-year volatility for both groups, with a particularly sharp spike in hit song danceability around 2017–2018. This aligns with the rise of electronic and dance-influenced pop music during that period. Our hypothesis that danceability predicts success appears well-founded based on these temporal trends.



In contrast, acousticness—the presence of acoustic versus electronic production—showed the clearest inverse relationship with hit status:





Non-hit songs had substantially higher acousticness levels (0.30–0.48) compared to hits (0.17–0.30). This difference suggests that acousticness is one of the strongest differentiators in our data. Songs that charted tended to be more produced and electronic, while more acoustic, folk-influenced, or singer-songwriter tracks rarely achieved mainstream success. This finding strongly validates our hypothesis that lower acousticness is associated with commercial success.




To understand how our audio features relate to one another and to hit status, we computed a correlation matrix of all numerical variables:





Several notable correlations emerge from this analysis:

Energy and Loudness (r = 0.82): These features are highly correlated, which makes intuitive sense—louder songs tend to be more energetic.
Acousticness and Energy (r = -0.73): A strong negative correlation confirms that acoustic songs tend to be quieter and less energetic.
Acousticness and Loudness (r = -0.67): As expected from the above, acoustic songs tends to be quieter.
Acousticness and Instrumentalness (r = 0.50): Songs with more acoustics tend to have a high instrumentalness rather than vocals.

-Valence and Danceability/Energy (r = 0.38): A slightly weaker correlation demonstrates that happier songs tend to have slightly higher levels of danceability and energy.
These correlations suggest potential multicollinearity issues in our data—some features are redundant predictors of a similar musical idea. This will be important to consider when building our models, as highly correlated predictors can increase variance and make it difficult to tell which predictors actually matter.



Our exploratory analysis provides strong preliminary support for our initial hypotheses:

Energy, danceability, and valence tend to be higher in hit songs
Acousticness tends to be lower in hit songs
Hit rates vary dramatically by genre, indicating that commercial success is partly driven by the music category itself. We will exactly to what extent after we run our models!
All features showed year-to-year fluctuations (e.g. energy levels between hits/nonhits seem to be converging), suggesting that the expectations for a hit may shift over time.
The correlation map suggests possible multicollinearity issues, which we’ll address during the modeling phase through model and feature selection.

Notably, both hit and non-hit songs showed a sharp increase in valence in 2019, suggesting a broader industry shift toward more upbeat music during that year. This also supports our hypothesis that positive, cheerful music performs better commercially.



Danceability, perhaps the most intuitive predictor of radio-friendly hits, showed strong separation between the two groups:





Hit songs were notably more danceable, with averages ranging from 0.59–0.67, compared to non-hits at 0.52–0.61. Interestingly, danceability showed more year-to-year volatility for both groups, with a particularly sharp spike in hit song danceability around 2017–2018. This aligns with the rise of electronic and dance-influenced pop music during that period. Our hypothesis that danceability predicts success appears well-founded based on these temporal trends.



In contrast, acousticness—the presence of acoustic versus electronic production—showed the clearest inverse relationship with hit status:





Non-hit songs had substantially higher acousticness levels (0.30–0.48) compared to hits (0.17–0.30). This difference suggests that acousticness is one of the strongest differentiators in our data. Songs that charted tended to be more produced and electronic, while more acoustic, folk-influenced, or singer-songwriter tracks rarely achieved mainstream success. This finding strongly validates our hypothesis that lower acousticness is associated with commercial success.




To understand how our audio features relate to one another and to hit status, we computed a correlation matrix of all numerical variables:





Several notable correlations emerge from this analysis:

Energy and Loudness (r = 0.82): These features are highly correlated, which makes intuitive sense—louder songs tend to be more energetic.
Acousticness and Energy (r = -0.73): A strong negative correlation confirms that acoustic songs tend to be quieter and less energetic.
Acousticness and Loudness (r = -0.67): As expected from the above, acoustic songs tends to be quieter.
Acousticness and Instrumentalness (r = 0.50): Songs with more acoustics tend to have a high instrumentalness rather than vocals.

-Valence and Danceability/Energy (r = 0.38): A slightly weaker correlation demonstrates that happier songs tend to have slightly higher levels of danceability and energy.
These correlations suggest potential multicollinearity issues in our data—some features are redundant predictors of a similar musical idea. This will be important to consider when building our models, as highly correlated predictors can increase variance and make it difficult to tell which predictors actually matter.



Our exploratory analysis provides strong preliminary support for our initial hypotheses:

Energy, danceability, and valence tend to be higher in hit songs
Acousticness tends to be lower in hit songs
Hit rates vary dramatically by genre, indicating that commercial success is partly driven by the music category itself. We will exactly to what extent after we run our models!
All features showed year-to-year fluctuations (e.g. energy levels between hits/nonhits seem to be converging), suggesting that the expectations for a hit may shift over time.
The correlation map suggests possible multicollinearity issues, which we’ll address during the modeling phase through model and feature selection.

title: MATH 244: Intermediate Data Science Project
text:
Hello! We are the Danish Club, made up of Vassar College senior mathematics majors Anders Hansen and Miles Sondergaard Jensen. Welcome to our MATH 244: Intermediate Data Science final project report. Navigate the tabs at the top to explore our analysis of what separates hit songs from non-hit songs!





Image from rawpixel

title: Methodology
text:
To predict song success, we implemented two classification models: LASSO (L1) and Ridge (L2) regression. These methods were chosen because of their ability to handle multicollinearity between musical features (which we observed between a few of the metrics) and prevent overfitting by penalizing large coefficients.


Our analysis was conducted using a Python-based pipeline integrated into our R environment via the reticulate package. The process involved several key stages:

Preprocessing: We used a column_transformer to handle different data types simultaneously.

Categorical Variables: ‘Key’, ‘Explicit’, ‘Mode’, and ‘Genre’ were processed using One-Hot Encoding.
Numerical Variables: All features on a 0-1 scale were passed through a StandardScaler to ensure all predictors were on the same scale.

Cross-Validation: To ensure our results were generalizable, we utilized 5-fold cross-validation during the model fitting process.
Data Splitting: The dataset was split into a Training Set (80%) to build the models and a Test Set (20%) to evaluate their final performance.




We tested both regularization techniques to see how they handled our feature set:

LASSO Regression: Useful for feature selection, as it can shrink the coefficients of less important variables all the way to zero.
Ridge Regression: Useful for dealing with highly correlated variables (like ‘Energy’ and ‘Loudness’) by shrinking coefficients proportionally without eliminating them.




We evaluated our models based on three primary metrics: * Accuracy: The percentage of correct hit/non-hit predictions.

F1 Score: The balance between precision and recall.
ROC-AUC: The model’s ability to distinguish between the two classes across various thresholds.

By comparing versions of these models with and without ‘Genre’ as a predictor, we aimed to isolate exactly how much the “sound” of a song matters versus its genre classification.
